region_of_origin: Teochew Chinese diaspora community, Singapore; origin: Chaozhou (潮州), Guangdong, China
food_subgroup: Sweet bean soup — split mung bean (dehulled) thickened with cornstarch or sweet potato starch, sweetened with rock sugar, served with you tiao (fried dough fritter)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for tau suan specifically. Closest parent concept: sweet legume dessert soup (mung bean-based). FoodOn does not currently enumerate Teochew-Singaporean dessert soups at dish level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for tau suan. Closest parent category: sweet dishes (A0010002). FoodEx2 does not enumerate this traditional Singaporean dish at the dish level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not contain a concept for tau suan or split mung bean sweet soup.

main_ingredients: split mung beans (dehulled green mung beans, 去皮绿豆)
you tiao (fried dough fritter — wheat flour, baking soda, salt, palm shortening)

serving.description: 1 bowl (~320g: ~200g split mung bean soup + ~120g you tiao)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-stall bowl of tau suan weighs approximately 320g, comprising about 200g of starch-thickened split mung bean sweet soup and approximately 120g of sliced crispy you tiao (fried dough fritter) placed on top.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Tau Suan / Split Mung Bean Sweet Soup; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mung beans (NDB 16080) and you tiao / Chinese cruller (NDB 18964) for composite calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g composite values derived from HPB Foodo tau suan entry and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy mung bean and you tiao component data. The 320g bowl composition (200g soup + 120g you tiao) is consistent with standard Singapore hawker portion. Values represent the blended composite per 100g of the full dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of split mung beans and composite dish estimation — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Split mung beans (dehulled) GI ~32 per University of Sydney GI Database. Composite dish GI estimated at ~52 due to: (1) cornstarch/sweet potato starch thickener raising GI above the bean baseline; (2) rock sugar (GI ~65) contribution; (3) you tiao (fried wheat dough, GI ~75) comprising ~37.5% of bowl weight. Weighted composite GI ~52 is consistent with low-GI classification. GL per 320g bowl = (52 × 62g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 32.2, rounded to 32.0 — classified HIGH GL (≥20). Without you tiao (200g soup only, ~29g available carbohydrate): GL ~15–18 (moderate-acceptable).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~380mg per 320g bowl); potassium from mung beans is beneficial
hypertension.guidance: Tau suan provides approximately 380mg sodium per standard bowl (119mg/100g × 320g), primarily from you tiao (baking soda and salt in dough); mung bean soup itself is very low sodium, and the potassium content (~416mg per bowl) from mung beans supports blood pressure regulation, making this dish acceptable for most hypertensive patients when consumed in standard portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL 32 per bowl with you tiao); cornstarch thickener and you tiao are primary GL drivers
type2_diabetes.guidance: With you tiao, tau suan carries a HIGH glycemic load of 32 per bowl and is not recommended for T2DM patients without modification; consuming the soup only (without you tiao) reduces GL to approximately 15–18 (acceptable), and requesting reduced starch thickness ('少粉, shǎo fěn') further lowers GL — the mung beans themselves are beneficial for glycaemic control (GI ~32) and the dish can be made T2DM-appropriate through these specific modifications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), zero cholesterol, high folate (50mcg/100g), prebiotic dietary fibre from mung beans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tau suan is low in saturated fat (2.6g per bowl) and contains zero cholesterol; the mung bean component provides 50mcg folate per 100g (supporting homocysteine metabolism) and 3.1g dietary fibre per 100g (prebiotic, supporting gut microbiome and cardiovascular health), making the soup component cardioprotective — you tiao is deep-fried and adds palm shortening fat, so limiting or omitting you tiao is advisable for patients with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prebiotic fibre from mung beans; high folate relevant for MTX-treated RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Split mung bean fibre (3.1g/100g) is prebiotic, supporting Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus populations linked to reduced RA disease activity; folate at 50mcg/100g is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate therapy, and the dish's zero cholesterol and low SFA profile are consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau suan (豆爽, dòu shuǎng in Teochew) is a traditional Singaporean and Teochew Chinese hawker dessert consisting of split mung beans (dehulled green mung beans, 去皮绿豆) cooked until soft in a sweet broth thickened with cornstarch or sweet potato starch, sweetened with rock sugar, and fragrant with pandan leaves. The dish is characteristically served topped with sliced crispy you tiao (油条, fried dough fritter). The dehulled mung beans produce a distinctive yellow-gold colour, distinguishing tau suan from whole-mung-bean green bean soup (which is clear and green). Texture is thick, starchy, and smooth with soft bean pieces; flavour is mildly sweet with a subtle pandan fragrance and a savoury-crispy contrast from the you tiao. A standard hawker bowl weighs approximately 320g (200g soup + 120g you tiao) and provides approximately 381 kcal, 13.1g protein, 8.6g fat, and 62.1g carbohydrate. Sodium per bowl is approximately 381mg. GI ~52 (low), but GL is HIGH at 32 per full bowl with you tiao — driven by the starch thickener and you tiao. Without you tiao, GL drops to approximately 15–18 (acceptable for T2DM). Folate is notably high at 50mcg/100g, relevant for MTX-treated rheumatoid arthritis patients. Mung beans are among the lowest-GI legumes (GI ~32). Tau suan is vegan, halal, and dairy-free. It is an increasingly rare old-school Singapore dessert, associated with hawker centres such as Maxwell Food Centre. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for mung beans and you tiao. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database (split mung beans GI ~32) with documented composite adjustment for starch thickener, rock sugar, and you tiao. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for tau suan composite dish not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (soup + you tiao) not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish is fully plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_a_mcg_rae reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no significant carotenoid or retinol sources in this dish). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish is fully vegan). vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D sources in this dish). GI ~52 is a composite estimate: split mung beans GI ~32 (University of Sydney GI Database), adjusted upward for cornstarch/sweet potato starch thickener and you tiao contribution. GL 32 per 320g bowl is HIGH (≥20); without you tiao GL ~15–18. Tau suan is an increasingly rare traditional Teochew-Singaporean hawker dessert; nutritional data reflects the standard preparation with you tiao as served at Singapore hawker stalls.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual local_names: chinese_simplified, chinese_traditional, malay, tamil, hindi. Restructured health_context to use required keys with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Added cardiovascular_disease health context. Populated aliases_by_language. Added gi_source as structured object. Documented all -1 sentinel values and confirmed-zero values in notes. Added nutrient_source as structured object. Mapped per_100g fields to RESULT template field names. Added ontology gap notes. Expanded natural_language_description. Added classification section.
Iteration 2: Added FoodEx2 code A0010002 (Sweet dishes) as closest_parent ontology anchor. Verified alias consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Performed exhaustive alias consistency self-check. Verified every string value in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in flat aliases array. Added 'dòu shuǎng' to flat aliases to ensure romanized entries are fully covered. Confirmed all 21 flat alias entries match aliases_by_language contents exactly. q8 gate now passes.
